metadata.summary: Comparison guide for vector databases (Qdrant, Weaviate, pgvector, Chroma, Pinecone, Milvus) with selection matrix and integration patterns.

content.title: Vector Database Comparison
content.summary: Comparison of popular vector databases and selection guide for RAG systems. Covers Qdrant, Weaviate, pgvector, Chroma, Pinecone, and Milvus across scale, hosting, filtering, and performance dimensions.

why.p: Selecting the right vector database early in a RAG project prevents costly re-architecture downstream. Different databases optimize for different constraints: Chroma for prototyping friction, pgvector for existing Postgres stacks, Qdrant for self-hosted production, Pinecone for fully managed scale, Weaviate for knowledge-graph workloads, and Milvus for massive distributed systems.

list.item: Starting a new RAG or semantic search project and selecting the vector DB technology
Migrating from a prototype DB (Chroma) to a production-grade one (Qdrant, Pinecone)
Evaluating whether to add a separate vector DB or extend an existing PostgreSQL setup with pgvector
Designing a multi-agent system where different agents use different retrieval backends
Database is already selected and deployed; comparison analysis adds no value at this stage
Corpus is < 10K vectors and latency is not critical — any DB works; pick the simplest
Evaluating operational cost in isolation from query patterns; benchmarks without real queries are misleading

section.name: when-to-use-details
section.p: Starting a new RAG or semantic search project and selecting the vector DB technology — early DB selection prevents costly re-architecture. Migrating from a prototype DB (Chroma) to a production-grade one (Qdrant, Pinecone) — prototypes outgrow single-node SQLite backing. Evaluating whether to add a separate vector DB or extend an existing PostgreSQL setup with pgvector — infrastructure decisions cascade through team ops burden. Designing a multi-agent system where different agents use different retrieval backends — matching agent patterns to DB capabilities improves throughput.

section.name: database-comparison-matrix
section.p: | Database | Best For | Hosting | Scale | Performance | Filtering | |----------|----------|---------|-------|-------------|-----------| | Qdrant | Production RAG | Self/Cloud | 100M+ | 41 QPS @ 50M | Payload filters | | Weaviate | Knowledge graphs | Self/Cloud | 10M+ | GraphQL native | Hybrid search | | pgvector | Existing Postgres | Self-hosted | 10M | Good w/ indexes | SQL WHERE | | Chroma | Prototyping | In-memory | 1M | Fast, simple | Metadata | | Pinecone | Managed scale | Cloud only | 1B+ | Serverless | Metadata | | Milvus | Large scale | Self/Cloud | 1B+ | GPU support | Attribute |

rule.statement: Default to Qdrant for new self-hosted production RAG systems; it has the best balance of performance, filtering, and operational simplicity.
rule.rationale: Qdrant exposes HNSW tuning knobs, supports payload-level filtering, and achieves 41 QPS @ 50M vectors with minimal ops overhead relative to managed solutions.

rule.statement: Use pgvector when the team already operates PostgreSQL and corpus is under 5M vectors — zero new infra, ACID transactions, SQL filtering.
rule.rationale: pgvector integrates seamlessly into existing Postgres stacks, eliminating the ops burden of a separate DB. HNSW and IVFFlat indexes provide sub-second queries on well-tuned configs.

rule.statement: Never use Chroma as the only vector store in production; it lacks replication, auth, and backup primitives.
rule.rationale: Chroma's in-memory mode loses data on restart. PersistentClient uses SQLite, which is single-writer only and prone to corruption under concurrent load.

example.title: pgvector setup with HNSW index
pgvector Python usage with psycopg2
example.code:
import psycopg2 from pgvector.psycopg2 import register_vector
# Connect and register vector type conn = psycopg2.connect("postgresql://user:pass@localhost/db") register_vector(conn)
cur = conn.cursor()
# Insert cur.execute(""" INSERT INTO documents (content, source, embedding) VALUES (%s, %s, %s) """, ("Document text...", "file.pdf", embedding))
# Batch insert from psycopg2.extras import execute_values
data = [(doc["text"], doc["source"], doc["embedding"]) for doc in documents] execute_values(cur, """ INSERT INTO documents (content, source, embedding) VALUES %s """, data)
conn.commit()

example.title: pgvector querying with cosine similarity
example.code:
# Cosine similarity (closer to 1 is better) cur.execute(""" SELECT id, content, source, 1 - (embedding <=> %s) AS similarity FROM documents WHERE category = %s ORDER BY embedding <=> %s LIMIT %s """, (query_embedding, "technical", query_embedding, 10))
results = cur.fetchall()
# L2 distance (smaller is better) cur.execute(""" SELECT id, content, embedding <-> %s AS distance FROM documents ORDER BY embedding <-> %s LIMIT 10 """, (query_embedding, query_embedding))
# Inner product (larger is better, for normalized vectors) cur.execute(""" SELECT id, content, embedding <#> %s AS neg_inner_product FROM documents ORDER BY embedding <#> %s LIMIT 10 """, (query_embedding, query_embedding))

example.title: pgvector Django integration
example.code:
# models.py from django.db import models from pgvector.django import VectorField, HnswIndex
class Document(models.Model): content = models.TextField() source = models.CharField(max_length=255) category = models.CharField(max_length=100) embedding = VectorField(dimensions=1536)
class Meta: indexes = [ HnswIndex( name="document_embedding_hnsw", fields=["embedding"], m=16, ef_construction=64, opclasses=["vector_cosine_ops"], ) ]
# queries.py from pgvector.django import CosineDistance
# Search similar = Document.objects.annotate( distance=CosineDistance("embedding", query_embedding) ).filter( category="technical" ).order_by("distance")[:10]

section.name: pgvector-performance-tuning
section.p: Performance tuning for pgvector: Set probes for IVFFlat to control accuracy vs. speed tradeoff; default is 1 (very fast, low recall). Set ef_search for HNSW to tune query time recall; default is 40. Create partial indexes for filtered queries where only specific categories are searched frequently. Always run VACUUM ANALYZE after bulk inserts to rebuild statistics; without it, the planner may choose bad query plans. Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to verify index usage and detect table scans on large collections.

example.title: IVFFlat and HNSW tuning

section.name: quantization-details
section.p: Quantization reduces memory footprint at the cost of recall accuracy: Scalar (int8) achieves 4x reduction with 1-2% accuracy loss — practical for most deployments. Product quantization (PQ) achieves 8-32x reduction with 3-5% loss — good for very large collections. Binary quantization achieves 32x reduction with 5-10% loss — only viable for high-dimensional models (> 768 dims) where recall tolerance exists. Always benchmark with your specific workload before quantizing production collections.

example.title: Pinecone index creation and querying
Pinecone querying with filtering

rule.statement: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World) is best for most use cases, balancing speed and accuracy. IVFFlat is better for faster build time when recall@10 > 0.95 is not required.
rule.rationale: HNSW provides better recall with tunable parameters (M for connections, ef_construction for build quality, ef_search for query quality). IVF builds faster on large datasets but requires careful tuning of probes vs. clusters.

section.name: hnsw-parameters
section.p: Parameters: - M: Number of connections per element (default: 16). Higher = better recall, more memory. Recommended: 12-48. - ef_construction: Search quality during build (default: 100). Higher = better index quality, slower build. Recommended: 100-500. - ef_search: Search quality at query time (default: 40). Higher = better recall, slower search. Recommended: 50-200.

section.name: ivf-parameters
section.p: Parameters: - nlist: Number of clusters (default: sqrt(n)). Higher = more clusters, better accuracy. Recommended: sqrt(n) to 4*sqrt(n). - nprobe: Clusters to search (default: 1). Higher = better recall, slower search. Recommended: nlist/10 to nlist/4.

section.name: quantization-comparison
section.p: | Method | Memory Reduction | Speed Impact | Accuracy Loss | |--------|------------------|--------------|---------------| | None | 1x | Baseline | None | | Scalar (int8) | 4x | Faster | 1-2% | | Product (PQ) | 8-32x | Faster | 3-5% | | Binary | 32x | Fastest | 5-10% |

section.name: selection-guide
section.p: Rapid prototyping → Chroma. Existing PostgreSQL → pgvector. Production self-hosted → Qdrant (recommended). Knowledge graphs → Weaviate. Fully managed → Pinecone. Massive scale (1B+) → Milvus or Pinecone.

section.name: selection-logic

rule.statement: Run a 3-day production load test before committing to a managed SaaS DB — cost curves are non-obvious under real query patterns.
rule.rationale: Public benchmarks use synthetic datasets; your domain query distribution may differ significantly. Managed SaaS costs scale non-linearly with vector count and query volume — modeling cost before committing prevents budget surprises.

example.title: Quick DB selector logic
DB requirements scorecard

section.p: Selection scorecard: corpus_vectors, latency_ms_p95, managed hosting, has_postgres, needs_graph, needs_hybrid_native, team_size (ops headcount).

rule.statement: M parameter (HNSW connections): Higher = better recall, more memory. Recommended: 12-48. ef_construction (build quality): Higher = better index quality, slower build. Recommended: 100-500. ef_search (query quality): Higher = better recall, slower search. Recommended: 50-200.
rule.rationale: HNSW tuning directly impacts recall-latency tradeoff. Choosing appropriate M, ef_construction, and ef_search values balances memory, build time, and query speed for your workload.

rule.statement: IVF parameters — nlist (clusters, default sqrt(n)): Higher = more clusters, better accuracy. Recommended: sqrt(n) to 4*sqrt(n). nprobe (search clusters, default 1): Higher = better recall, slower search. Recommended: nlist/10 to nlist/4.
rule.rationale: IVF is faster to build than HNSW on large datasets but requires careful tuning of clustering granularity and search depth. Choosing nprobe based on recall targets prevents silent performance degradation.

section.name: limitations-and-constraints
section.p: Public benchmarks (ANN Benchmarks, VectorView) use synthetic datasets; your domain query distribution may differ significantly. Managed SaaS costs (Pinecone, Weaviate Cloud) scale non-linearly with vector count and query volume — model the cost before committing. pgvector IVFFlat recall degrades without periodic VACUUM ANALYZE — often omitted in naive benchmarks. HNSW index rebuild time is O(n log n) — for 100M+ vectors, re-indexing takes hours; plan for blue/green index strategy. Chroma's in-memory mode has no persistence; data loss on process restart is a common agent-side bug. Milvus requires dedicated infrastructure (etcd, MinIO, Pulsar) — operational burden is high for small teams.

list.item: Default to Qdrant for new self-hosted production RAG systems; it has the best balance of performance, filtering, and operational simplicity.
Use pgvector when the team already operates PostgreSQL and corpus is under 5M vectors — zero new infra, ACID transactions, SQL filtering.
Never use Chroma as the only vector store in production; it lacks replication, auth, and backup primitives.
Run a 3-day production load test before committing to a managed SaaS DB — cost curves are non-obvious under real query patterns.
Index type choice: HNSW for high-recall retrieval; IVFFlat for faster build time when recall@10 > 0.95 is not required.
Apply scalar quantization (int8) for corpora > 10M vectors to reduce memory by 4x with < 1% recall loss.
Always store the raw vector alongside metadata in a backup store (PostgreSQL or S3) — vector DBs are not the source of truth.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: A database-selector subagent is invoked once at project initialization. It receives the corpus size, query latency requirement, team infrastructure, and hosting preference, then returns a ranked recommendation with rationale. For production systems, a benchmark subagent runs a representative sample of real queries against 2–3 candidate DBs and reports P50/P99 latency and recall@10 before the final decision is committed.

list.item: db-selector — Analyzes requirements (scale, hosting, filtering, latency) and outputs DB recommendation with rationale.
benchmark-runner — Loads sample data into candidate DBs, runs query benchmark, returns recall and latency comparison table.
migration-planner — Given current DB and target DB, generates a migration plan including re-embedding schedule and downtime estimate.

Agents that read DB benchmark articles choose the winner of the latest public benchmark — which may not reflect your corpus distribution. Always run your own benchmark with your actual query distribution.
Chroma's default persistence mode uses DuckDB+Parquet; concurrent write access from multiple agent processes causes corruption — use client-server mode.
pgvector requires SET ivfflat.probes = N per connection to improve recall — agents using connection pools may not set this, silently getting recall@10 of 0.6 instead of 0.95.
Pinecone serverless pricing charges per read unit (RU); a single query(top_k=100) costs 10x more than top_k=10 — agents that default to large top_k blow the cost budget.
Dimension mismatch: embedding model changes produce vectors of different dimension; inserting into an existing collection fails silently in some clients — validate dimension at insert time.

Human-in-loop checkpoint: DB migration (changing vector DB technology) requires re-embedding the full corpus — validate that the new embedding model produces semantically equivalent results on a sample before cutting over.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: | Tool | Purpose | Install / docs | |------|---------|----------------| | qdrant-client | Qdrant Python client | pip install qdrant-client / qdrant.tech/documentation | | weaviate-client | Weaviate v4 Python client | pip install weaviate-client / weaviate.io/developers | | chromadb | Chroma Python client | pip install chromadb / docs.trychroma.com | | pinecone | Pinecone Python client | pip install pinecone / docs.pinecone.io | | pgvector | PostgreSQL vector extension + Python adapter | pip install pgvector psycopg2 / github.com/pgvector/pgvector | | pymilvus | Milvus Python client | pip install pymilvus / milvus.io/docs |

section.name: services-and-apps
section.p: | Service | Type (SaaS/OSS) | Agent-friendly? | Notes | |---------|-----------------|-----------------|-------| | Qdrant Cloud | SaaS | Yes | Free 1GB cluster; REST + gRPC; strong filtering | | Qdrant OSS | OSS | Yes | Docker single-node; Kubernetes cluster mode | | Weaviate Cloud | SaaS | Yes | 14-day free sandbox; GraphQL native | | Weaviate OSS | OSS | Yes | Knowledge graph + hybrid search | | Pinecone | SaaS | Yes | Serverless tier; best for 1B+; no self-hosted | | Chroma | OSS | Yes | Dev only; no production SLA | | pgvector | OSS | Yes | PostgreSQL extension; excellent for existing PG stacks | | Milvus | OSS / Cloud | Yes | GPU support; enterprise scale; complex ops |
